Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I didn't really have a set schedule—I'd study in bursts, usually when I felt the pressure building up or when I absolutely had to. With my part-time job and limited resources at home, I couldn't always sit down when I wanted to. I'd often end up studying late at night when everyone was asleep and the wifi wasn't being shared. It wasn't organized at all, but somehow I made it work for exams.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I wouldn't touch anything course-related, and then other weeks I'd cram everything in a few days. Deadlines were basically the only thing that dictated when I studied. Regular times weren't really possible with my schedule and everything else I had to juggle.
Q3: Usually it was panic that got me to log in—like realizing an assignment was due soon or that I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd get an email notification and that would remind me the course existed. I rarely logged in just to browse or stay on top of things proactively. It was always reactive, never really planned.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd go weeks without logging in, then suddenly be on there constantly for a few days before a deadline. I tried to make it routine at the start of the semester, but life got in the way and I just couldn't maintain it. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped with my anxiety a bit, but they didn't change my LMS habits.
Q5: The reading materials and lecture notes were the most useful because I could download them and study offline. I'm a read-write learner, so having actual text I could highlight and annotate worked best for me. The videos were okay but I didn't always have the bandwidth to stream them properly. Anything I could save and access later without internet was gold.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums and a lot of the optional readings. The discussions felt pointless to me—like people just posting to get participation marks rather than actually discussing anything meaningful. I was also bored by a lot of the supplementary materials because they felt repetitive. When you're short on time and motivation, you cut what seems least essential.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be on the exam or in the assignments. I needed to see the payoff clearly. If something felt like busywork or extra fluff, I couldn't bring myself to care about it. With limited hours to study, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones at least had consequences if I didn't do them, so they got prioritized. Optional stuff just fell off my radar entirely because there was no immediate penalty for ignoring it. When you're already struggling to keep up, optional feels like a luxury you can't afford.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download the PDFs and readings, print what I could at the library, and study from those. I also used my own notes heavily and looked things up on YouTube or other sites when I didn't understand something. The LMS was just where I grabbed materials, not where I actually learned.
Q10: Most of my exam prep wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd spend hours going through my handwritten notes, re-reading printed materials, and testing myself on concepts. None of that leaves a digital trace. I also studied with a friend sometimes, quizzing each other, and obviously that's invisible to any system tracking my online activity.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my whole approach. I'd log in once, download everything I needed, and then not come back until I needed something new. Streaming videos or reading things online wasn't practical for me with my internet situation. So one login might represent days of actual studying that happened offline.
Q12: There were definitely times when everything felt impossible. My anxiety would spike, especially around midterms, and I'd just freeze up and avoid the course entirely. Financial stress at home made it hard to focus on academics. Some weeks I was just surviving, not thriving, and the course felt like this overwhelming thing I couldn't face.
Q13: Completely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to look at. Confusion about assignment requirements made me procrastinate even more because I didn't know where to start. The competing responsibilities were constant—I couldn't just be a student, I had to be everything else too.
Q14: Oh, definitely. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open but I'd just be staring at the screen, not absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things. I'd scroll through materials without really reading them. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety made it worse—the more important something was, the more terrifying it felt to start. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with its own problems. It's a terrible cycle but I couldn't break out of it easily.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. They were the only thing that could override my avoidance and anxiety. I'd basically ignore the course until a deadline was looming, then go into crisis mode and get things done. My LMS activity probably looks like nothing, nothing, nothing, then a huge spike. That's just how I operated.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. When I got good feedback, it motivated me briefly, but that feeling faded fast. When feedback was critical or I got a lower grade than expected, it made me want to avoid the course even more. The response time on my work was slow sometimes, so by the time I got feedback, I'd already moved on mentally.
Q18: The course structure was pretty rigid, which didn't work great for my chaotic life. Weekly modules with set deadlines assumed you had consistent time to dedicate, which I didn't. I would've done better with more flexibility. The structure felt like it was designed for students with stable schedules and resources.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made things easier—that was a lifesaver. But the attendance expectations and the way participation was tracked made things harder because I couldn't always be present online or in person. The course assumed a level of consistent engagement that just wasn't realistic for my situation.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways—logging in, looking around, maybe downloading things, but then life gets in the way of actually completing work. Or they might be confused about what's expected and spending time trying to figure things out without producing submissions. High activity doesn't mean productive activity.
Q21: That's literally me. My exam score was really high even though my LMS activity was low. I studied hard, just not in ways the system could see. I'm good at understanding material when I actually sit down with it, and I test well. The LMS only shows one narrow slice of what engagement looks like.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline work, the thinking you do while commuting, the discussions with friends. They definitely can't capture your mental state or what's going on in your life that affects your learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just going through motions. It's when you're curious and thinking critically, not just checking boxes. For me, engagement happened in bursts—when I could focus and the anxiety wasn't overwhelming. It's a mental state, not a metric.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling with anxiety and financial stress while still managing to learn the material. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed notes or the genuine interest I had in some topics despite my low attendance. It also wouldn't show how the mindfulness intervention helped me cope, even if it didn't change my login patterns.
